shot.subject: water
shot.notes: dead calm; the lake a perfect mirror at first light

segments.text: Every world keeps one story it tells quietly. In this garden — a world of half-centimeter citizens and meter-tall grass — the quiet story is a paradox: the largest resident in the water is not a fish at all. It is a mammal, like us.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: from high above, a vast dark shape glides beneath the surface

segments.text: From the air it reads as a moving piece of the lakebed. A shadow with lungs.

shot.subject: whale
shot.notes: the flank slides past camera, endless, smooth and gray

segments.text: The whale.

shot.subject: whale
shot.notes: running alongside the whale at depth, its full length in frame

segments.text: Everything a fish is, the whale is not. It is warm-blooded, holding its own heat in the cold water. It does not lay eggs — it bears live young and nurses them on milk. And though it spends its life submerged, it cannot breathe the water at all.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: the surface bulges; the back breaks through, a spout of mist blows off

segments.text: Watch it rise. On top of the head sits a blowhole — a nostril that opens straight to a pair of lungs. Every so often the giant must climb to the ceiling, empty those lungs in a single blast, and refill them. It holds one breath for as long as it can, then trades it for another. The whale's tether is not to the deep. It is to the air.

shot.subject: whale
shot.notes: a slow half-circle around the head; the eye tracks the camera

segments.text: Its ancestors were land animals that walked back into the water tens of millions of years ago. Look closely and the history shows — a mammal's skeleton, hidden hip bones, a spine that drives the tail up and down, not side to side like a fish. The whale did not come from the fish. It went to join them.

shot.subject: whale
shot.notes: the great mouth opens, curtains of fringe hang from the upper jaw

segments.text: The world's whales split into two great kinds by how they feed. One kind carries no teeth at all. In their place hang plates of baleen — stiff fringed curtains that strain the water. They gulp an enormous mouthful, push it back out, and swallow whatever the sieve holds: clouds of tiny drifting animals, caught by the millions. The blue whale, largest animal that has ever lived, eats this way.

shot.subject: whale
shot.notes: the whale sweeps low through a drifting haze of fine motes

segments.text: The other kind keeps its teeth — the toothed whales: the dolphins, the orcas, the sperm whale. They hunt one prey at a time, and they hunt with sound.

shot.subject: whale
shot.notes: macro on the broad forehead, angled toward the dark

segments.text: A toothed whale sends out a stream of clicks and listens for the echoes — building a picture of the black water from returning sound. It is echolocation, the same trick the bats use in the night air, run here in water where it carries even better. In the lightless deep, the whale sees with its voice.

shot.subject: whale
shot.notes: the tail stock flexes; the fluke rises and presses down, slow and total

segments.text: Now the engine. That great horizontal tail — the fluke — beats up and down, driven by the largest muscles the animal owns. One stroke moves a mass no predator in this water could hope to challenge. Speed, at this size, is momentum with good manners.

shot.subject: underwater
shot.notes: the whale crosses the full frame, dwarfing the sunlit water column

segments.text: And it is far from silent. Whales are among the loudest and most far-carrying voices in nature. Some of their calls travel enormous distances through water, and the males of certain kinds sing long, patterned songs that shift and repeat across a season. Sound is how a giant keeps in touch across a country too wide to see.

shot.subject: underwater
shot.notes: from the lake floor, the whale passes overhead like an eclipse

segments.text: From the floor, its passing is an eclipse — the light dims, the water shoves sideways, and every smaller life keeps its distance until the sky clears again.

shot.subject: underwater
shot.notes: glide through the stirred water behind the fluke, motes swirling

segments.text: Here is why the whale matters far beyond its size. A whale feeds in the deep and releases its waste near the surface — and that waste is fertilizer, rich in iron and nutrients the sunlit water is starved of. Those nutrients feed the drifting plants at the base of everything. Scientists call it the whale pump: the giant lifts fertility up from the dark to where the light can use it.

shot.subject: whale
shot.notes: the whale banks along the drowned slope, fluke nearly brushing the silt

segments.text: Like the worms beneath the meadow, the giant is a plowman of its world. Stirring the water, moving the nutrients, tending a pasture it will never see bloom. The garden does love its hidden gardeners.

shot.subject: whale
shot.notes: the eye, close and calm, unhurried

segments.text: And its gift outlasts its life. When a great whale finally dies and sinks, its body becomes a feast on the barren floor below — a whale fall, feeding a whole crowd of deep-water creatures for years from a single passing. In life it fertilizes the top of the water. In death it seeds the very bottom.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: the lake from above; its one deep region reads darker than all the rest

segments.text: Its whole kingdom hangs on a single detail of the sculpture. When the Gardener pressed the land low, one hollow went deeper than all the rest — and that accident of the hand is the whale's entire habitat. Shallow water it can visit. Deep water is the only place it can be.

shot.subject: underwater
shot.notes: the whale hangs motionless mid-pocket, perfectly neutral, barely drifting

segments.text: Sometimes it simply stops, hanging weightless in its one deep room. Whales are long-lived and slow-breeding, and in the real world we very nearly erased them — hunted to the edge of vanishing before the killing was called off. Many kinds are climbing back now, one slow generation at a time. A giant recovers the way it does everything else: without hurry.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: sunset; the whale's slow wake draws one line across the golden lake

segments.text: There is only one, here — one giant for one lake, a sky of fish for neighbors, and the moon overhead moving its whole world. Somewhere below the sleeping garden, the largest heartbeat in this world keeps its own patient time.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: pull back and up: lake, meadow, and treeline in one twilight frame

segments.text: We have now met every wild citizen of this garden — the crawlers, the fliers, the swimmers, and the one air-breathing leviathan among the fish. But one resident remains, and he is the strangest of all.

shot.subject: soldier
shot.notes: the inch-tall human stands at the shore, looking out over the water

segments.text: He walks upright. He carries a mind. And in a world of towering grass, he stands exactly one inch tall. Next: the Visitor.
